era_context: The late 14th century was a period of significant political and military realignment across Eurasia. The Yuan Dynasty was in its final years in China; the Ottoman Empire was rising in the Balkans; the Hundred Years' War continued in Europe; and Central Asia was experiencing the rise of Timur. This was also a period of religious reform movements and the beginning of the end of the medieval period in Europe.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English forces conduct campaigns in France
events.description: The Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with English forces conducting military campaigns and raids in France. By 1367, English power was still significant, though the period of English dominance was beginning to wane. The war would continue for another century, with shifting fortunes for both sides. This period saw the consolidation of English holdings in Aquitaine and Gascony.
events.certainty_note: The Hundred Years' War is extensively documented in contemporary chronicles and administrative records. The year 1367 is well-attested.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces internal instability in China
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty in China was facing increasing internal instability and rebellion in 1367. The Mongol-led Yuan Dynasty was weakening due to administrative corruption, economic problems, and widespread peasant rebellions. By 1367, the Ming rebellion under Zhu Yuanzhang was gaining strength and would soon overthrow the Yuan Dynasty in 1368. This period marked the final years of Mongol rule in China.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang (Ming rebel leader, future Hongwu Emperor)
events.certainty_note: The decline of the Yuan Dynasty is well-documented in multiple sources. The year 1367 is clearly attested as part of the period of instability leading to the dynasty's fall in 1368.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates control in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Murad I (r. 1362–1389), was consolidating its control over Anatolia and beginning to expand into the Balkans. By 1367, the Ottomans had established themselves as a major power in the region. This consolidation would accelerate in the following years, establishing the Ottoman Empire as a dominant force in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested, though specific dates for individual campaigns vary by source. The year 1367 is a reasonable approximation for the period of consolidation.

events.title: Timur consolidates power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), a Turco-Mongol warrior, was consolidating his power in Central Asia around 1367. By this year, he had begun to defeat rival khans and establish himself as the dominant power in the region. Over the next few years, he would create one of history's largest empires. This period marked the beginning of the Timurid era and the rise of Central Asian power.
key_figures: Central Asian khans and tribal leaders

sources.name: Timurid chronicles and later compilations (e.g., Zafar-nama by Sharaf ad-Din Ali Yazdi)

events.certainty_note: Timur's early career is attested in later sources, but the exact chronology is uncertain. The year 1367 is an approximation for his consolidation of power.

events.title: Papacy in Avignon; Urban V serves as Pope
events.description: Pope Urban V (r. 1362–1370) was serving as pope in 1367, with the papacy still based in Avignon. Urban V was attempting to reform the church and address the issues of papal authority and the location of the papal seat. This period saw growing calls for the pope to return to Rome and increasing tensions over church reform. Religious reform movements were gaining momentum in England and elsewhere.
events.certainty_note: The papacy of Urban V in 1367 is well-documented. The year 1367 is clearly attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of the Yuan Dynasty's decline is well-documented, with no significant disconfirming evidence for 1367 being a year of instability. However, some sources emphasize that the Yuan Dynasty's problems were longstanding and not unique to 1367. The exact chronology of Timur's early campaigns is disputed in modern scholarship, with some sources placing his consolidation of power in different years. Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested, but the significance of specific campaigns has been debated by historians.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1367 is moderately well-documented for Europe and China, with detailed chronicles and administrative records. The decline of the Yuan Dynasty is well-attested in multiple sources. Ottoman and Central Asian history relies more heavily on later compilations, creating some chronological uncertainty. The sources that survive are often shaped by court patronage and the interests of literate elites. African, American, and Oceanic history for this year is almost entirely absent from surviving written records. The Eurocentric and Sinocentric bias in historical preservation is particularly evident for this period.

graph_edges.note: Part of the ongoing Hundred Years' War
Yuan Dynasty instability led to the Ming Dynasty establishment in 1368
